1: Wayyosep ’iyyov se’et mishlo (Job again lifted up his mashal and said) — nasa’ = to lift/carry; mashal = a wisdom saying, discourse, parable, extended metaphor. The mashal is not merely a speech but a heightened wisdom form — an authoritative utterance with didactic weight. The same formula appears at Job 29:1 (wayyosep ’iyyov se’et mishlo), framing Job 27–31 as one extended mashal block. The repetition signals that Job is still in the role of wisdom teacher, not just a sufferer complaining.
2: Hai-’el ’asher hesar mishpati (as God lives, who has denied me justice) — the hai-X oath formula (“as X lives”) is the most solemn Hebrew oath form, typically invoking YHWH or the king. Here Job swears by God even as he accuses God of the very injustice he is protesting. The verb sur = to turn aside/deviate — YHWH has turned aside from giving Job justice. Veshaddai ’asher-hemer nafshi (and the Almighty who has embittered my soul) — marar = to be/make bitter (same root as Naomi’s name Mara, Ruth 1:20, and the bitter herbs of Passover, Exodus 12:8). Job is bitter-souled but still invoking God.
3: Ki-khol ’od nishmat-’eloah be’appi (as long as the breath of God is in my nostrils) — nishmat ’eloah = the breath of God, directly echoing Genesis 2:7 (nishmat hayyim, breath of life) where YHWH breathes into Adam’s nostrils. Job’s oath has a double irony: he swears by the same God who sustains his life by that very breath, even while accusing God of injustice. Veruah ’eloah be’appi = “and the spirit/breath of God in my nostrils” — the parallel phrase reinforces the divine-origin-of-breath theology underlying Job’s oath.
4: Im-tedabberan sefatay ’avlah (if my lips should speak wickedness) — ’avlah = iniquity/injustice/wrongness, the quality of something morally skewed. Im-yehgeh leshoni remiyyah = “if my tongue should utter deceit/treachery” — remiyyah = deceit, slackness, treachery (from the root of ramah = to deceive). Job’s lips and tongue form the merism of speech: neither will deviate from truthful integrity.
5: Hallilah li im-’atsdiqet ’etkhlem (God forbid that I should justify/agree you are right) — hallilah is a strong interjection: “far be it from me!” / “God forbid!” (used 21 times in OT for strong moral rejection). ’atsdiq = declare right/justify (hiphil of tsadaq); Job refuses to justify his friends’ position against him. Im-’agva tummati mimmenni = “I will not surrender my wholeness from me” — tummah = integrity/wholeness (noun of the tam family, same as 1:1 and Job 2:3,9). The word “surrender” (sur) = turn aside, depart — he will not let his integrity be moved even by death.
6: Betsdaqati hehezaqti velo-’arpennah (I hold fast to my righteousness and will not release it) — tsedaqah = righteousness (noun form, distinct from the adjective tsaddiq). hazaq = to hold fast, grip firmly (the same verb as Job’s wife’s rhetorical question at Job 2:9, hahaziq betummatekha). Lo-yihrap levavi miyamay = “my heart does not reproach me for any of my days” — harap = to reproach, taunt (pi’el). Job’s conscience (lev = heart, the seat of inner knowledge) is clear for every day of his life.
7: Yehiy ke-rasha’ ’oyevi (let my enemy be as/like the wicked) — jussive form, an imprecatory wish. Job invokes the fate he is about to describe (the wicked man’s doom, vv8–23) on those who oppose him. The friends argue that Job’s suffering proves his wickedness; Job turns the argument: let those who falsely accuse him receive the lot they attribute to the wicked. The imprecation is not vengeance but the inversion of the friends’ logic.
8: Ki mah-tiqwat hanef ki-yivtsa’ (what hope does the godless one have when God cuts him off?) — hanef = the godless/profane person (from hanap = to be polluted, pollute; applied to land, people, speech). ki-yivtsa’ ’eloah nafsho = “when God extracts/cuts off his soul” — batsa’ = to cut off, extract (as cutting a root or stopping a flow); the image of God cutting the thread of the godless person’s life. The godless man’s hope is zero at the moment of divine cutting-off.
9: Hatsaqato yishma’ ’el (will God hear his cry?) — tsaqah = to cry out (the cry of the oppressed that reaches God, Exodus 3:7; Nehemiah 5:1; Job 34:28). The godless man’s lack of covenant relationship means his cry does not have the standing to reach God. Ki tavo ’alav tsarah = “when trouble comes upon him” — the godless can only expect that his trouble-cry falls into deaf cosmic ears, because he has not cultivated the relationship that gives the cry its standing.
10: Im-’al-shaddai ya’ang (does he delight in the Almighty?) — ’anag = to be soft/delicate, to take delight in (only a few occurrences; cf. Psalms 37:4, hit’anag ’al-YHWH = delight yourself in YHWH). yiqra’ ’eloah bekhol-’et = “does he call on God at all times?” — the two marks of genuine piety that the godless lack: delight in the Almighty and constant prayer. Both are marks Job himself displayed (Job 1:1,5), which is why his prayers and cries have standing where the godless person’s do not.
11: ’Orekh bi yad-’el (I will instruct you about the hand of God) — the verb is yarah (hiphil) = to throw/shoot/direct — the same root as Torah (teaching/direction). Job positions himself as a teacher of divine wisdom; the “hand of God” (yad-’el) is the metaphor for God’s active power and governance in history. ’asher im-shaddai lo-’akhahed = “what is with the Almighty I will not hide” — the verb khahad = to hide/conceal (pi’el). Job claims a kind of prophetic boldness: he will not conceal what he has seen of God’s ways.
12: Hen-’attem kullekem hazitem (you yourselves have all seen/beheld it) — hazah = to see, behold (often of prophetic/visionary seeing, more intensive than the ordinary ra’ah). Job charges the friends with willful selective blindness: they have seen the same evidence as Job but draw convenient conclusions. Vema-zeh hevel tehbalu = “why then have you become so thoroughly vain/empty?” — hevel = breath/vapor/vanity (the key word of Ecclesiastes, used 38 times there). The friends’ wisdom has devolved to hevel — empty, meaningless, like a puff of breath.
13: Zeh-heleq-’adam ra’sha’ me-’el (this is the lot of the wicked from God) — heleq = portion, lot (the share assigned to someone). The description of the wicked’s fate in vv13–23 is linguistically and theologically close to what the friends have argued throughout. Many commentators consider this section to have been accidentally relocated from a friend’s speech, or to represent Job presenting his opponents’ argument before refuting it. venahalt ’aritsim yiqqahu mishaddai = “and the inheritance of oppressors they receive from the Almighty” — ’aritsim = tyrants/oppressors (from ’arats = to terrify, inspire dread).
14: Im-yirbu vanav le-harev (if his children multiply, it is for the sword) — the multiplication of children (normally a sign of blessing, Genesis 22:17; Psalms 128:3) becomes a multiplication of victims. Vetse’etsa’av lo-yisbe’u-lahem = “his descendants will not be satisfied with bread” — tsotse’et = offspring (a rare form, also in Isaiah 22:24). The wicked’s family suffers the consequences of his deeds.
15: Seridav bammavet yiqberu (his survivors will be buried by death/plague) — serid = survivor, remnant. Mavet = death, or possibly a disease-demon (the Ugaritic Mot, death personified). Ve’almanotav lo-tivkeyyna = “and his widows will not weep for them” — the absence of mourning is a sign of ultimate disgrace in the ancient Near East (cf. Jeremiah 22:18 for the fate of those denied mourning).
16: ’Im-yitspor ke’afar kesep (though he pile up silver like dust) — tsaphar = to pile up, heap. The comparison of silver (kesep) to dust (afar) emphasizes the quantitative abundance; so much silver it becomes as cheap and plentiful as dust. Vekikhomer yakhim levush = “and lay up clothing as plentiful as clay” — khomer = clay, mud (the building material as cheap as dirt); clothing stored in such abundance it is like a pile of clay.
17: Yakhim vetsaddiq yilbash (he may prepare it but the righteous will wear it) — the wicked man is the inadvertent benefactor of the righteous. Vekhesep naqqi yehaleq = “and the innocent will share out the silver” — the accumulated wealth of the wicked will be redistributed to the innocent by divine reversal. This is the Deuteronomic principle of possessing the property of the defeated wicked (Deuteronomy 6:10–11).
18: Banah kea’ash beito (he builds his house like a moth builds) — the ash (moth) builds its cocoon, which is destroyed at a touch. Ukhesuka ’asah note = “and like the shelter (sukkah) a watchman puts up” — sukkah = a temporary booth/shelter (the same word as the Feast of Booths); the watchman’s field shelter is deliberately temporary, built to be abandoned. The wicked man’s house, however grand, is actually a moth-cocoon or a harvest-booth: structurally temporary, destined for dissolution.
19: ’Ashir yishkav velo-ye’asef (the rich man lies down but is not gathered) — ne’esaf ’el-’ammav (gathered to his people) is the standard Hebrew expression for a peaceful, honored death and burial among family (Genesis 25:8; Job 35:29; Job 49:33). The negation: the wicked rich man lies down in death but is not gathered — denied the dignified death of the righteous. Yifqah ’enav ve’einennu = “he opens his eyes and he is gone” — one blink of waking and the man is erased.
20: Tassigehu kemayim ballahot (terrors overtake him like a flood) — nasag (hiphil) = to overtake, reach; ballahot = terrors (from balal = to terrify). Laylah genavathu sufah = “in the night a storm steals him away” — ganav = to steal (the same root as the vision stealing in Job 4:12, where Eliphaz said the revelation “stole” to him). The wicked man is stolen in the night, as suddenly as a thief enters.
21: Yis’ahu qadim vayelekh (the east wind sweeps him away and he goes) — qadim = the east wind, the hot, destroying wind of the Levant (sirocco), associated with YHWH’s judgment (Exodus 14:21; Ezekiel 17:10). Visarhu mimmiqomo = “and it hurls him from his home” — sara’ = to blow away, toss, scatter. The east wind that gave Job his initial disaster (Job 1:19, “a great wind from the wilderness”) reappears here as the wicked man’s destroyer.
22: Veyashlekh ’alav velo-yahemol (for God hurls his power at him without mercy) — shalakh = to throw, hurl (God as the one who hurls against the wicked). Lo-yahemol = without pity (the verb hamal = to spare, have compassion; negated here). Baroah yivrah miyaddo = “he flees desperately from his hand” — the wicked man’s futile flight from the divine assault.
23: Yispoq ’alav kappayim (men clap their hands at him) — the hand-clapping here is not applause but the gesture of derision or astonishment (cf. Lamentations 2:15; Ezekiel 25:6 where clapping of hands = contempt). Veyisroq ’alav mimqomo = “and hisses him from his home” — saraq = to whistle/hiss (a gesture of contempt, Jeremiah 19:8; Zephaniah 2:15). The wicked man’s departure from his home is marked by public contempt: clapping and hissing, the sounds of dismissal.
